todays_post.title: Thursday March 13 | Sales | SLAY | Familiar | CTA: B
todays_post.content:
# Thursday March 13 | Sales | SLAY | Familiar | CTA: B

![Post Image](2026-03-13-dad-story-image.png)

---

My dad never understood what I do.
Last week he finally did.

He's 72. Retired engineer. Practical man.

For 20 years, every time someone asked him what his son does, he'd say: "Something with computers."

He wasn't wrong. He just couldn't picture it.

Last week, he came to visit.

I showed him one screen.
Not a presentation. Not a diagram.

A live dashboard showing patient flow across 15 hospitals in 3 countries.

Real-time bed occupancy. Clinical alerts. Pending discharges. Revenue per episode.

All feeding from systems that didn't talk to each other 18 months ago.

He stared at it for a full minute.

Then he said: "So you built the nervous system."

That's it.
That's the job.

I've spent 20 years building nervous systems for organizations.

At Intel, it was the network infrastructure connecting LTE towers.
At PaySky, it was Egypt's first SuperApp connecting payments, loans, and insurance in one platform.
At Talabat, it was the operations layer that scaled from 30,000 daily orders to 7 million.
At Network International, it was the PMO connecting 300+ banking projects across 8 countries.

And now: a $50M transformation connecting 15 hospitals across 3 countries.

Every one of those jobs had a different title.
None of them had a simple answer to "what do you do?"

But my dad got it in 30 seconds.

Because the job was always the same.

**Find the broken connections. Build the ones that matter.**

The title changes. The work doesn't.

What's the clearest someone has ever described complex work back to you?

..

By the way, I've been documenting my journey managing a $50M hospital transformation across 3 countries. If you're navigating a similar challenge, happy to share what's working. Drop a comment or DM.

#Leadership #DigitalTransformation #HealthTech #Career #GCC

posts.ready_comment: That shift from builder to orchestrator sounds clean in theory, but the real challenge is the cultural leap. I've been through this myself building a 14-product AI automation ecosystem, and honestly? It wasn't the tech that was hard. It was getting experienced engineers to actually trust the agent enough to let go. What I've learned is that orchestration needs a completely different leadership style. So I'm curious: how are you helping your senior talent make that psychological shift?

posts.title: #executivesearch #leadershiphiring #ksa #uae #gcc #vision2030 ...
posts.author: Michael Meegan 98139B98
posts.topic: 🔎 The rightleadershipappointment does more than fill a role – it defines direction, safeguards legacy, and shapes the future of an organisation. At SearchMode, we cultivate enduring ...
posts.ready_comment:
That point about leadership appointments really hits home. I've spent the last few years steering a $50M digital transformation across 15 hospitals at Saudi German Hospital Group, and here's what I've learned: the technology is actually the easy part. Getting everyone aligned around a single vision when each department has its own agenda? That's where the real work happens.

The question I keep asking myself is this: does your appointment create momentum that outlives you? Or does everything revert back once you move on? That's the true test of leadership, in my view. Not whether you hit the milestones, but whether the direction you set becomes part of how the organization actually operates, not just another item on a project timeline.
